coredata.dc:title: A highly efficient molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for assessing whole wheat grains by the rapid and sensitive detection of alkylresorcinols
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Yangyang
Li, Jie
Zhao, Shichao
Fan, Haoran
Li, Hongyan
Wang, Jing
Sun, Baoguo
coredata.dc:description: To differentiate whole wheat foods from refined wheat foods is still challenging grain industry and confusing consumers. Alkylresorcinols (ARs), as biomarkers of whole wheat grains, can serve for assessing the authenticity of whole wheat foods. Herein, a highly efficient fluorescence sensing platform (CDs@MIP) for rapid and sensitive analysis of ARs was explored, using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorophores and 5-heneicosylresorcinol (C21:0 AR) as template molecules embedded in a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) coating. Benefiting from the specific cavities in the probe and a photo-induced electron transfer effect, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP was significantly quenched in the presence of C21:0 AR, exhibiting a superior binding efficiency and selectivity. As a result, the fabricated optical sensor delivered a wide linear range of C21:0 AR from 0.015 to 60 μg mL−1 with an ultralow detection limit of 4 ng mL−1. It was noteworthy that the sensor was successfully applied for the rapid detection of C21:0 AR in commercial whole-wheat foods as well as visualization analysis on the test paper, comprehensively validating the practicality and efficacy of CDs@MIP based fluorescence assay. The study provides a rapid and sensitive detection method of C21:0 AR, paving a new way for guiding grain industry to effectively qualify the authenticity and to quantify the content of whole wheat in wheat-based foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Whole wheat foods
Alkylresorcinols
Fluorescence sensor
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Optosensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs, CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP
Pretreatment and analysis of commercial whole wheat samples
Fabrication and application of fluorescent test paper
Fluorescence and absorption behavior of CDs@MIP towards C21:0 AR
Establishment of fluorescence detection method for C21:0 AR
Visualized fluorescent test paper for C21:0 AR detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of as-prepared CDs@MIP. (A) TEM image and (B) particle size distribution of the CDs. (C) SEM and (D)TEM images of CDs@MIP. (E) SEM and (F) TEM images of CDs@NIP. EDS analysis of the (G) CDs@MIP and (H) CDs@NIP. (I) FTIR spectra of samples.

(A) UV–visible absorption and PL emission spectra of the CDs dispersion. (B) PL spectrum of CDs@MIP before and after the addition of 60 μg mL−1 C21:0 AR under the 480 nm excitation. (C) PL responses of CDs@MIP dispersed in different polar solvents. (D) A schematic illustration of the possible quenching mechanism.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the CDs@MIP sensor in the presence of C21:0 AR at varied concentrations (0–60 μg mL−1). (B) The calibration curve relationship between F0/F of CDs@MIP and the logarithm of C21:0 AR concentration. (C) Fluorescence responses of the CDs@NIP at varied C21:0 AR concentrations. (D) The calibration curve relationship between F0/F of CDs@NIP and the logarithm of C21:0 AR concentration.

(A) Chemical structures of C21:0 AR and its analogues. (B) The fluorescence responses of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP in the addition of 60 μg mL−1 C21:0 AR and its analogues. The effect of foreign co-existing analogues with 1- and 8-fold concentrations on the fluorescence quenching of CDs@MIP: C21:0 AR concentrations at (C) 0.5 μg mL−1 and (D) 40 μg mL−1.

(A) Construction steps of the paper sensor with a specific BTBU pattern and the corresponding fluoresce responses in the absence or presence of C21:0 AR. (B) Photographs of the paper sensor under visible (top) and 365 nm-UV light (down) dropped with different concentrations of C21:0 AR.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedures of the CDs@MIP.

head.ce:title: A highly efficient molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for assessing whole wheat grains by the rapid and sensitive detection of alkylresorcinols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Beijing Technology and Business University (BTBU), 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Beijing Technology and Business University (BTBU), 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China-Canada Joint Lab of Food Nutrition and Health (Beijing), School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University (BTBU), 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China-Canada Joint Lab of Food Nutrition and Health (Beijing)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China-Canada Joint Lab of Food Nutrition and Health (Beijing), School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University (BTBU), 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To differentiate whole wheat foods from refined wheat foods is still challenging grain industry and confusing consumers. Alkylresorcinols (ARs), as biomarkers of whole wheat grains, can serve for assessing the authenticity of whole wheat foods. Herein, a highly efficient fluorescence sensing platform (CDs@MIP) for rapid and sensitive analysis of ARs was explored, using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorophores and 5-heneicosylresorcinol (C21:0 AR) as template molecules embedded in a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) coating. Benefiting from the specific cavities in the probe and a photo-induced electron transfer effect, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP was significantly quenched in the presence of C21:0 AR, exhibiting a superior binding efficiency and selectivity. As a result, the fabricated optical sensor delivered a wide linear range of C21:0 AR from 0.015 to 60 μg mL−1 with an ultralow detection limit of 4 ng mL−1. It was noteworthy that the sensor was successfully applied for the rapid detection of C21:0 AR in commercial whole-wheat foods as well as visualization analysis on the test paper, comprehensively validating the practicality and efficacy of CDs@MIP based fluorescence assay. The study provides a rapid and sensitive detection method of C21:0 AR, paving a new way for guiding grain industry to effectively qualify the authenticity and to quantify the content of whole wheat in wheat-based foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Health benefits of whole wheat foods have been extensively studied in recent years, unveiling that intake of whole wheat grain was consistently associated with decreased risk of type 2 diabetes, cancers or other chronic diseases (Aune et al., 2013; Calinoiu and Vodnar, 2018). The demonstrated health benefits have promoted consumption of whole wheat foods. However, it is unclear how much whole wheat is present in commercial wheat products due to the lack of a standardized definition and metrics for whole wheat content (Fan et al., 2022a; Killilea et al., 2020). To differentiate whole wheat foods from refined wheat foods is still challenging for grain industry and confusing for customers.

Alkylresorcinols (ARs), also named 5-n-alkylresorcinol, are a group of long-chain phenolic compounds characteristic with a 1, 3-dihydroxyalkylbenzene phenolic ring with different lengths of alkyl chains varying from 17 to 25 carbons (Fan et al., 2022b). ARs specifically exist in the outer layers of the kernels of wheat, rye, barley, and other grains. Therefore, they are commonly used as the biomarker for whole wheat foods (Mohamad et al., 2019; Oriente et al., 2020). Quantification of ARs paves an effective way for assessing the amount of whole wheat in foods. Up to date, colorimetric techniques based on colored derivatives from ARs coupled with diazotized Fast Blue (B and RR) salts have been utilized as the main analytical protocols over the past years (Bernal et al., 2016a; Gajda et al., 2008); moreover, chromatographic method (HPLC and GC-MS) has emerged for ARs detection in the human plasma (Linko et al., 2002) and cereals (Ross and Kochhar, 2009), exhibiting obvious advantages of high reproducibility and reasonable sensitivity. Recently, an electrochemical assay has also been proposed as a potential approach for ARs determination in terms of redox characteristics of hydroxyl groups on the benzene ring (Yang et al., 2021). However, the disadvantages of high cost, low sensitivity, time-consuming, and tedious operation procedures greatly hinder applications of these methods, making them not appropriate for the rapid and portable analysis of ARs (Li et al., 2020; Rodriguez-Morato et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2010). To this end, the establishment of a rapid and sensitive analytical method for ARs determination is of practical significance and urgently desired in whole-wheat food industry.

As a promising alternative to traditional methods, fluorescence sensors have emerged and employed in the fields of pollutant detection, fluorescence imaging, and medical diagnosis (Scognarniglio et al., 2014). By introducing a luminophore whose fluorescence characteristics (fluorescence intensity or fluorescence color) change upon target analyte binding, the fluorescence sensors can detect the analyte contents in complicated systems (Zhu et al., 2022). Based on this principle, a wide variety of luminophores were synthesized to acquire desired fluorescence sensing performances (Yang et al., 2018). Therein, carbon dots (CDs), as a new category of fluorescent nanomaterials, have received considerable attention in the construction of optical sensors, for their simple synthesis, low cost, good photostability and biocompatibility, as well as strong luminescence characteristics (Liu et al., 2020). However, the CDs-based probe often presents low selectivity, as its fluorescence response not only occurs in the presence of target analytes but also other analogues (Yuan et al., 2017).

Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) is a type of synthetic materials with specific recognition sites that can selectively rebind target molecules in preference to other related compounds (Rutkowska et al., 2018). In general, these recognition sites are created by the copolymerization of a functional monomer and cross-linker in the presence of template molecules (target analyte); after elution, specific cavities with the same shape, size and functional groups to templates are left, and consequently can rebind with templates selectively (Panagiotopoulou et al., 2016). Benefiting from the strong affinity, high selectivity, and easy preparation of MIP technology, the MIP-capped CDs sensors have been widely exploited for analysis of pollutants, pesticides or macromolecules (Dabrowski et al., 2018; Ren and Chen, 2015; Zhen et al., 2018). In this connection, it is conceivable that the quantitative detection of ARs with high selectivity and sensitivity can be achieved by a rational design of MIP-capped CDs sensor. Yet, there has been no reported work on the synthesis of ARs-based MIP or the applications of ARs-based fluorescence sensors in assessing of whole wheat grains.

Inspired by the above situation, for the first time, a novel fluorescence sensing platform (CDs@MIP) was constructed and applied to quantify the biomarker of whole wheat foods via a facile molecular imprinting strategy. For molecularly imprinting, C21:0 AR, the most dominant homologue of ARs in whole wheats, was selected as template molecules (Ross et al., 2004; Zarnowski et al., 2000), and arylamide was used as functional monomer to achieve efficient identification of C21:0 AR. The fluorescence properties of CDs@MIP were systematically investigated, and the optosensing mechanism was discussed; furthermore, the proposed sensor was also applied to quantify C21:0 AR content in commercial wheat products.

Standard samples of 5-heneicosylresorcinol (C21:0 AR) and 5-heptadecylresorcinol (C17:0 AR) were received from Kaimu Technical Service Center (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, acetic acid and urea were purchased from Signopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acrylamide (AM), 2,2′-Azobis (2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), Vitamin E (VE), ferulic acid (FA) and 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHBA) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Wheat flour, whole wheat flour, bran and whole wheat breads were purchased from the local supermarket. All chemicals were analytical-reagent grade and used as received. Deionized water (Mili-Q Direct-Q water purification system, Millipore, USA) was used throughout the experiments.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL JSM 7401, Japan) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA) were applied to reveal the morphologies of as-synthesized samples. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy were performed by a Vertex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a UV/vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies Cary 100, USA). Fluorescence responses were acquired on a multifunctional microplate reader (Synergy H1, Biotek Instruments Inc., USA). C21:0 AR concentrations in commercial food samples were also evaluated using a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with LC-20A SPD-20A columns (GL Sciences Inc., Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared according to a previously reported method (Qu et al., 2012) with some modifications, as provided in the Supporting Information.

The CDs@MIP nanospheres were prepared via a simple imprinting method. First, 10 mg of CDs was dispersed in 1 mL ethanol and sonicated for 30 min to obtain a uniform dispersion (10 mg mL−1). Then 30 μL the CDs dispersion was pipetted into 20 mL ethanol containing 0.12 mmol C21:0 AR and 0.6 mmol AM, which was pre-polymerized for 1 h with continuous nitrogen gas and placed in dark conditions. Sequentially, 2.4 mmol EGDMA and 30 mg AIBN were added into the reaction system, followed by heating at 60 °C for 24 h under continues nitrogen protection. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation and repeated washing with ethanol and distilled water. Subsequently, the sediment was further eluted with the acetic acid/methanol mixture (v/v, 1:9) to completely remove C21:0 AR templates. After drying at 40 °C under vacuum overnight, the final CDs@MIP was obtained. Additionally, the non-imprinted polymer (CDs@NIP) was also prepared in a manner identical to CDs@MIP but in the absence of C21:0 AR.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a multifunctional enzyme plate analyzer with excitation wavelength at 420 nm and methanol as the solvent. For the optical response time measurement, 2 mg of CDs@MIP was dispersed into 2 mL of C21:0 AR solution (0.5 μg mL−1), followed by oscillation in the dark and successively detection of fluorescence intensity within 60 min. For fluorescence assays, a series of C21:0 AR standard solution (0.015–60 μg mL−1) containing 1 mg mL−1 CDs@MIP were employed, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded immediately after 10-min incubation. The obtained fluorescence responses F
0
/F was plotted, where F
0 and F denoted the fluorescence intensities of CDs@MIP in the absence and presence of C21:0 AR, respectively. The detection limit of CDs@MIP (in ng·mL−1) was calculated based on the formula of LOD = 3ẟ ⁄ n, where LOD is the detection limit, δ for standard deviation of the intercept and n for the slope of the calibration curve (Dai et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2018). To conduct the selectivity and competition experiments, fluorescence responses of CDs@MIP towards C21:0 AR, its analogues (C17:0 AR, VE, FA and DHBA) as well as their mixtures (C21:0 AR mixed with 1- and 8-fold analogues) were recorded. The stability of as-constructed CDs@MIP was also measured in the dark for a successive 30-day period using a 365 nm UV lamp. To reduce errors, each experiment was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and analysis of commercial whole wheat samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability of as-established assay, 5 different whole wheat samples with different C21:0 AR levels were selected for ARs quantification. To obtain a limpid extract, 1 g of actual sample (bread slices need to be crushed) and 40 mL methanol were first mixed under intensively stirring for 30 min and centrifuged, with the supernatant being collected and further filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. For fluorescence detection, the CDs@MIP (1 mg mL−1) was dispersed in the above extract solution, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded after a 10-min incubation. After analysis, we calculated the detected C21:0 AR content in the form of μg·mL−1 based on the volume of extracted solution as well as the corresponding C21:0 AR mass in ug·g−1 based the mass of whole wheat solids.

For comparison, standard high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was also implemented with some modifications (Patzke et al., 2016). In details, the wheat extract was first purified by a silica gel column using a chloroform/diethyl ether (9:1, v/v) solution, and subsequently purified by a silicone reversed-phase C18 solid phase extraction column (SPE). Prior to SPE, it was activated with 4 mL each of methanol and ultrapure water. Then 5 mL extraction solution was loaded into the extraction column, rinsed with 4 mL volume fraction of 10% methanol solution, and eluted with 5 mL methanol. The eluent was blown dry by nitrogen, diluted into 5 mL with methanol, and filtered through 0.22 μm microporous membrane for further HPLC-UV analysis. Sample analysis was performed at 40 °C using an HPLC Inertsil ODS-SP C18 column (4.6 mm × 250 mm id, 5 μm) at 280 nm ultraviolet wavelength. The elution gradient (eluent A, water; eluent B, methanol) was set as 96% of eluent B, and injection volume was 10 μL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and application of fluorescent test paper
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescence test paper based on CDs@MIP was fabricated according to the following steps: 100% cotton fiber filter paper was cut into specific shapes, evenly dropped with CDs@MIP solution (1 mg mL−1), and naturally dried in the dark. The as-prepared test paper was stored in a dark, dry, and ventilated place at room temperature. For applications, C21:0 AR solutions at varied concentrations (0.015–60 μg mL−1) were dipped onto the test paper, placed in dark for 10 min, and then the pictures of the paper were taken under a UV light at 365 nm.

The fabrication procedures of CDs@MIP were illustrated in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the green luminescent CDs with an average diameter of 8.5 nm (Fig. 1 A and B) were prepared via a low-cost and one-step hydrothermal method. The quantum yield of CDs was calculated to 18.6% (Table S1), and their luminescence stability was distinct, without obvious decay over 30 days (Fig. S1). Concomitantly, C21:0 AR, the dominant AR component in the whole wheats (Ross et al., 2004; Zarnowski et al., 2000) was chosen as template, and AM was used as functional monomer to form strong H-bond interactions with templates, with AIBN as initiator and EGDMA as crosslinking agent. After the precipitation polymerization and a subsequent elution process, a uniform MIP layer with specific C21:0 AR cavities was decorated on the surface of CDs, forming the CDs@MIP nanospheres. When recombined with C21:0 AR, the recognition cavities in CDs@MIP will specifically rebind to C21:0 AR, and consequently result in a significant signal output on the fluorescence intensity.

The morphology of as-prepared CDs@MIP was first characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As predicted in Fig. 1C and D, CDs@MIP showed a uniform spherical structure with an average diameter of 760 nm (Fig. S2A), while the control polymer, CDs@NIP, had a similar spherical morphology but with a slightly larger particle size of approximately 830 nm (Fig. 1E–F and Fig. S2B). Obviously, the diameters of two nanospheres were apparently larger than that of CDs (only 8.5 nm), indicating that a thick polymer layer was successfully coated on the surface of CDs and the CDs@MIP composites was obtained. As shown in Fig. 1C, the nanospheres presented a rough surface, suggesting the existence of numerous cavities in polymer layers due to the imprinting process. Moreover, the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis revealed that the two nanocomposites had a similar chemical composition (Fig. 1G and H), but displayed a slightly higher N content (10.6 wt%) in the CDs@MIP, compared to its control sample (9.8 wt%). It is speculated that the slightly higher level of AM (functional monomer) was introduced during the imprinted polymerization due to the interaction with C21:0 AR.

In order to ascertain the synthesizing and surface functional groups, FTIR spectrometry of the CDs, CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP were implemented, as displayed in Fig. 1I. The CDs presented characteristic peaks at 3453 cm−1, 3367 cm−1, and 1704 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O–H, N–H, and CO (from carboxylic groups) bands, respectively (Huang et al., 2018). These peaks confirmed that plenty of amino and carboxyl groups existed on the surface of CDs, which could easily form H-bond interactions with the –OH groups in template molecules and then provide specific binding sites with templates (Lv and Gao, 2020). In contrast, the CDs@MIP displayed various new characteristic peaks that belonged to the imprinted polymer. For example, the adsorption peaks at 2981 cm−1 (methyl –CH3), 2953 cm−1 (methylene –CH2), 1729 cm−1 (CO) and 1253 cm−1 (ester group COOR) represented the existence of EGDMA, while the absorptions at 3367 cm−1 (N–H stretching) and 1390 cm−1 (C–N stretching) were stemmed from functional monomer AM. Meanwhile, as predicted in an enlarged FT-IR region in Fig. S3, the characteristic band of CDs (O–H stretching at 3453 cm−1) was also detected in the CDs@MIP, further identifying the successful cladding of CDs in polymer layers. Notedly, the abundant surface groups on CDs could also interact with functional monomer via noncovalent bonds, such as van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions, and hydrogen bonding, resulting in a solid interface between CDs and polymer (Ling et al., 2022). In addition, the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs@NIP was almost the same with that of CDs@MIP, confirming their similar chemical structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and absorption behavior of CDs@MIP towards C21:0 AR
ce:section.ce:para: To detect the fluorescence responses of the CDs@MIP, the fluorescence intensity changes before and after the addition of C21:0 AR were recorded. As presented in Fig. S4, with the addition of C21:0 AR, the emission intensity of CDs@MIP at 520 nm significantly decreased and a decay of up to 35.7% of its initial intensity was noted. In contrast, the value for CDs@NIP was only 8.6%. It demonstrated that the proposed CDs@MIP fluorescence sensor had prominent superiority and good feasibility for the detection of C21:0 AR.

To gain further insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of as-constructed sensor, the UV–visible absorption and PL emission spectra of the CDs and C21:0 AR were investigated. Generally, four main reasons are assigned for the fluorescence quenching (Yu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2011): (i) Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET), (ii) inner filter effect (IFE), (iii) photo-induced electronic transfer (PET) and (iv) formation of ground-state complex. As shown in Fig. 2 A, a large proportion of the UV absorption spectrum of CDs coincided with that of C21:0 AR, but no new peaks or peak migration were observed after the C21:0 AR addition, indicating that the ground state coordination reaction did not occur between the two components. It suggested that the formation of ground-state complex were not responsible for the quenching of CDs. Furthermore, in a FRET, the spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of luminophores and the UV–vis absorption of analytes was required, but no overlapping between CDs and C21:0 AR were detected, suggesting that FRET did not cause fluorescence changes in CDs. The IFE, actually, is the re-absorption of the excited/emitted light of the fluorophore by quencher (Zhang et al., 2019). In this respect, an excitation wavelength of 480 nm was applied to elute the newly produced absorption area of CDs with C21:0 AR (Fig. 2B). The result revealed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP was extremely decreased due to the inclusion of C21:0 AR, indicating the negligible effect on the quenching efficiency of C21:0 AR. Thus, IFE can be excluded for the quenching mechanism.

To investigate the effects of polarity of solvent, fluorescence intensities of CDs@MIP dispersed in various solvent was measured (Fig. 2C). The polarity of solvent did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the luminescent significantly, and thus was not the reason for its quenching. Based on the above analysis, we speculated that a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process may occur between the CDs and C21:0 AR. Specifically, the CDs served as electron donors, transferring excited electrons from the conduction bands of CDs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of C21:0 AR under photoinduced conditions, and the fluorescence was quenched accordingly, as illustrated in Fig. 2D. On the basis of a tunneling pathway model, the distance dependence of electron transfer can be evaluated, where jumps through hydrogen bonds was expected for our PET process (Sisido et al., 2001). Similar fluorescence quenching and PET mechanism were also evident on other CDs-based sensor for the detection of bisphenol A (Zhang et al., 2021) and pyrethroids (Zhu et al., 2021), without significant change in the lifetime of CDs towards quenchers.

The adsorption equilibrium of polymers provides essential information on the specific recognition towards analytes. To assess the recognition ability of CDs@MIP, the static and dynamic adsorption plots of two nanospheres were analyzed. As predicted in Fig. S5A, the adsorption capacity (Q) of CDs@MIP increased from 0.04 mg g−1 to 131.14 mg g−1 when the C21:0 AR concentration ascended from 0.015 μg mL−1 to 60 μg mL−1. Nevertheless, the data for CDs@NIP were significantly smaller, with the maximum Q value of only 39.90 mg g−1. Apparently, the significant Q value obtained at the high initial concentration might be caused by the high probability of collision between C21:0 AR with adsorbent surfaces and the high rate of C21:0 AR diffusion onto adsorbents. Plus, the large adsorption gaps between CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP were also obtained at the high initial concentrations. Explicably, at low C21:0 AR levels, extensive nonspecific adsorption may happen on the polymers surface, leading to a small adsorption gap between two composites; at a high C21:0 AR level, a large amount of C21:0 AR would specifically adsorb into the tailor-made cavities of MIP, resulting in a large difference in Q values. Correspondingly, imprinting factor IF, an important indicator of recognition specificity, was calculated, reaching 3.29 at the C21:0 AR concentration of 60 μg mL−1. As listed in Table S2, the IF value of CDs@MIP was among the highest values achieved for other phenolic substances or hydroxyl containing chemicals (Gryshchenko and Bottaro, 2014; Kan et al., 2008; Lu et al., 2019). Furthermore, the Q data with different incubation times were recorded to unveil the diffusion kinetics of CDs@MIP (Fig. S5B). Apparently, the Q value increased quickly at the first 10 min and then slowly reached its equilibrium, implying that a fast diffusion kinetics could be obtained during the C21:0 AR rebinding. The high adsorption capacity, good imprinting factor as well as fast diffusion kinetics of the CDs@MIP sensor further elucidated the applicable potentials for the selective and rapid C21:0 AR detections.

Detection conditions have a profound impact on the fluorescence responses of CDs-based sensors. Prior to the titration study, influences of detection parameters, including molar ratios of template to crosslinker, CDs volumes, CDs@MIP concentrations and incubation time on the PL quenching were studied.

In the imprinting process, crosslinker, essential to immobilize the functional monomers around template molecules, has a potent ability in affecting the adsorption and selectivity of polymers. As presented in Fig. S6A, the molar ratio of template to crosslinker (AR/EGDMA = 1: 10, 1: 20, and 1:40) was first investigated. It was revealed that the fluorescence response F0/F was the most prominent at the ratio of 1:20, whereas a lowered ratio of AR/EGDMA may cause the instability of imprinted structure and an extremely high ratio can decrease the number of recognition sites per unit mass of CDs@MIP. Thus, the ratio of 1:20 was selected as the optimal condition for subsequent experiments.

As a luminophore, the dosage of CDs can significantly influence the analytical performance of the sensor. As shown in Fig. S6B, the F0/F raised gradually with the increased volume of CDs from 10 μL to 30 μL, but swiftly decreased thereafter, which may be associated with a reduced thickness of MIP layer and the correspondingly decreased number of binding sites. Additionally, the fluorescence changes with varied CDs@MIP concentrations (0.25–2.5 mg mL−1) were also recorded in the presence of 50 μg mL−1 C21:0 AR (Fig. S6C). Clearly, the fluorescence response (F0/F) increased with the increasing concentrations, but plateaued at 1 mg mL−1. It is inferred that a relatively high concentration of luminescent nanomaterials may lead to their self-aggregation in the case of fixed volume of solvent. Moreover, the optimized incubation time for C21:0 AR adsorption was monitored. As revealed in Fig. S6D, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP decreased significantly at the first 10 min, but remained unchanged after 10 min, revealing that the CDs@MIP can reach its adsorption saturation in a short period (only 10 min), thus enabling rapid detections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of fluorescence detection method for C21:0 AR
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration-dependent luminescent properties of the sensing system were measured by exposing CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP nanospheres to a varied concentrations of C21:0 AR. As shown in Fig. 3 A, at an elevated C21:0 AR level, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP at 520 nm gradually decreased, which can be easily observed by naked eyes under UV light irradiation (Insert image of Fig. 3A). The significant linear relationship between F0/F and the logarithm of C21:0 AR concentration was well-fitted into the equation: F0/F = 0.055 lnCC21:0 AR+1.317 (R2 = 0.998), with a wide linear concentration range of 0.015–60 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3B). Detection limit (LOD) is an important parameter to assess the sensitivity of analytical methods. It refers to the lowest analyte concentration and corresponds to the minimum detected signal (Wen et al., 2020). The calculated LOD for CDs@MIP was only 4 ng mL−1, verifying that the fabricated fluorescence sensor can be a promising method for the trace quantification of C21:0 AR in complex systems. To put insight into the conditions under which analytical signals were generated in the proposed system, the molar ratio of analyte (C21:0 AR) to the marker (CDs) was calculated, as listed in Table S3. The molar ratio was only 0.00006 at the detection limit of 4 ng mL−1, but it was linearly correlated to detection limit at the range of 0.015–60 μg mL−1, reaching 0.9 at the C21:0 AR concentration of 60 μg mL−1. Henceforth, the fluorescence response F0/F still slightly increased but with no linear correlation (potentially approached its maximum signal output).

Compared to the previously reported C21:0 AR detection protocols that were summarized in Table S4, our sensor displayed remarkable advantages in analytical performances. The CDs@MIP sensor is comparable or even superior to the ranges obtained by previous works (Bernal et al., 2016b; Geerkens et al., 2015; Ross, 2012). Moreover, the detection limit in this work (4 ng mL−1) is notedly lower as compared to other studies. As the control group, the fluorescence responses of the CDs@NIP were also conducted. As displayed in Fig. 3C and D, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@NIP showed a similar increasing trend on the addition of C21:0 AR, but the quenching efficiency was significantly lower than that of CDs@MIP. The quenching constant Ksv (the slope of the calibration curve) is an important indicator to estimate the sensitivity of detection assays. As calculated from the Stern-Volmer formula, the Ksv of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP were 0.055 and 0.014, respectively, which suggested that the C21:0 AR-tailored cavities in CDs@MIP increased the absorption capacity of C21:0 AR and thus significantly boosted the sensitivity of the fluorescence sensor.

To evaluate the selective specificity of as-proposed fluorescence sensor, several possible co-existing structural analogues were evaluated under the same conditions, including C17:0 AR, VE, FA and DHBA, as illustrated in Fig. 4 A. It was obvious that C21:0 AR had a significantly higher F0/F response than other analytes (Fig. 4B), which was attributed to the tailor-made cavities by molecular imprinting and manifested the high selectivity of as-developed CDs@MIP (Yang et al., 2018). Plus, compared to CDs@MIP, the fluorescence response of CDs@NIP lacked selectivity to all analytes with almost similar F0/F values, signifying the absence of specific recognition sites on NIP layers. For the anti-interference experiment, the mixed solutions containing C21:0 AR and 1- or 8-fold analogues were conducted, unveiling that the quenching efficiency of these interferences were almost negligible (Fig. 4C and D). As a direct study of cross-reactivity, the C17:0 AR, a representative ARs homologue that has four less carbon atoms in the alkyl chain than C21:0 AR but exhibited a notedly higher bioactivity, was selected, with its individual as well as mixed fluorescence responses being evaluated (Fig. 4B–D). C17:0 AR had a slight quenching effect on the luminophores mainly due to the structure similar to that of C21:0 AR, but still showed a significantly lower quenching efficiency and consequently an inconspicuous effect on the fluorescence response in its mixed solution (C17:0 AR + C21:0 AR). To this end, it was proved that the designed sensor exhibited excellent selectivity and anti-interference to C21:0 AR and can be applied to realize precise identification in commercial samples.

In addition, the F0/F values of CDs@MIP were also assessed by a successive removal-rebinding cycle test to reflex its reproducibility (Fig. S7). After five repeated experiments, the detection performance was almost the same as that in the initial test, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 1.2%, illustrating its excellent reproducibility. Moreover, the optical stability of the CDs@MIP was investigated by recording fluorescence intensities at 10 min interval, showing no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity under a continuous UV irradiation within 60 min (Fig. S8A). For the sample of CDs@MIP kept at 4 °C for 30 days, the fluorescence intensity only decreased 0.52% of its original figure (Fig. S8B), further confirming the excellent photobleaching resistance of the CDs@MIP probe.

To verify the applicability of CDs@MIP-based sensor, the analysis of C21:0 AR in five different commercial samples (grain flour and whole grain products, S1–S5) were conducted. The extract solutions of samples after pretreated in methanol for 30 min were measured by CDs@MIP sensor (Fig. S9A), showing the apparent fluorescent quenching. For comparison, the extract solutions were also conducted using a standard HPLC method (Fig. S9B–D).

As listed in Table S5, when the extract had a low C21:0 AR level, the concentration cannot be detected using a HPLC method, such as the blank solutions in samples S1 and S4 with a relatively high LOD (0.2 μg mL−1) and a high initial linear concentration (1 μg mL−1). In contrast, these extracts were easily detected by the CDs@MIP sensor with an ultralow LOD (4 ng mL−1) and a wide linear range (0.015–60 μg mL−1), firmly identifying the superiority of the CDs@MIP for trace C21:0 AR detections. Furthermore, the spike and recovery experiments were performed by the addition of a given concentration of C21:0 AR (3 μg mL−1). The collected C21:0 AR contents from the CDs@MIP sensor, with recoveries in the range of 98.79–103.23% and the RSD of 0.54–3.20%, which were in accordance with that from the HPLC method. Therefore, it is disclosed that the proposed CDs@MIP sensor rendered a satisfactory sensing platform for the quantitative determination of C21:0 AR in complex commercial samples.

Wheat bran (S2) had the highest C21:0 AR content, while refined wheat flour (S1) showed the lowest value, disclosing a close correlation between C21:0 AR levels and contents of whole wheat grains in foods. However, the quality of whole wheat products in the market is not guaranteed due to the lack of relevant whole wheat food standards (Hoevenaars et al., 2020). For instance, the C21:0 AR contents of sample S4 and S1 were similar, indicating that the whole wheat bread S4 is a fake product almost entirely made of refined wheat flour. In contrast, another whole wheat bread (S5) had a six-times superior C21:0 AR content to S4, suggesting more real whole wheat flours included in the formula of sample S5. Ultimately, it is evident that the fluorescence sensor can be a promising candidate for C21:0 AR detection to replace the traditional methods, in terms of its operational simplicity, low cost, trace detection and high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualized fluorescent test paper for C21:0 AR detection
ce:section.ce:para: The exploration of advanced fluorescence sensing microdevices is of great promise in realizing the rapid and sensitive point-of-care testing and in-field diagnosing. Therefore, a fluorescence test paper based on CDs@MIP was proposed for the visualized C21:0 AR detection on site. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the paper sensor trimmed with a specific “BTBU” pattern was successfully fabricated, and emitted bright and uniform green fluorescence under dark condition. However, with the addition of the C21:0 AR solution onto the paper, the fluorescent intensity became darker within several minutes and can be easily distinguished by the naked eyes. Moreover, a series of paper sensors with different C21:0 AR concentrations were constructed, as shown in Fig. 5B. Notably, the luminous intensity of the paper gradually decreased with the increasing concentrations of C21:0 AR from 0 to 60 μg mL−1, which can be further analyzed by color recognition software. For example, by employing an ImageJ software, the color changes of the paper sensor can be translated into digitized results, realizing the visual and facile C21:0 AR analysis. However, we should note that the actual C21:0 AR adsorption time in the test paper was far less than 10 min (the optimal adsorption time in Fig. S6D), mainly resulting from the rapid volatilization of methanol solvent and leaving unabsorbed C21:0 AR molecules on the surface of test paper. Thus, it is predictable that further enhancement in the fluorescent response of the test paper could be obtained by extending the adsorption time or modifying the soaking method. All the results verify that the construction of CDs@MIP-based microdevices is feasible and promising for the rapid, visualized, and sensitive detection of C21:0 AR.

In this study, a highly efficient fluorescence sensor based on CDs@MIP was synthesized for the rapid and sensitive detection of C21:0 AR. The CDs@MIP exhibited a superior adsorption specificity upon C21:0 AR, and the constructed sensor delivered remarkable sensing performances, with a wide linear range from 0.015 to 60 μg mL−1 and an ultralow detection limit of 4 ng mL−1. Remarkably, the fluorescence sensor was successfully applied for the detection of C21:0 AR in commercial whole wheat samples and for the construction of a visualized paper sensor, comprehensively validating the practicality and efficacy of our CDs@MIP based assay. This novel optical sensor with its simplicity, high sensitivity, superior selectivity, and visual features, can be a promising method for qualifying the authenticity and quantifying the content of whole wheat in wheat-based foods.

Yangyang Wen: Data curation, Validation, Supervision, Writing – original draft, paper draft review and revision. Jie Li: Experiment conduction, Methodology, Resources. Shichao Zhao: Experiment conduction, Methodology. Haoran Fan: Experiment conduction, Methodology, Resources. Hongyan Li: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – original draft, paper draft review and revision. Jing Wang: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Revision. Baoguo Sun: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Paper editing, Revision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (32102010, 32172236), Cultivation Project of Double First-Class Disciplines of Food Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology & Business University (No. BTBUYXTD202206), and Discipline Construction-Food Science and Engineering (SPCX-2022003).
